schools.summary: Henry: David's fear is the effect of weak faith; consulting his own heart, not the ephod or prophet, he forsakes the land where God set his standard.
A gross distrust of God's promise after repeated deliverances; Clarke calls the despairing conclusion a very hasty one.
Ellicott: a dull despair robbed David of faith and hope, with no prayer or consultation; Keil notes the flight to the Achish before whom he once feigned madness.
Gill: a fit of unbelief into which even the man after God's own heart could fall.
Achish son of Maoch is the king of 1 Samuel 21 and 1 Kings 2 (a fifty-year reign); the six hundred are the nucleus of a now far larger band.
Clarke: not one circumstance is free from blame; record is not approval, and here David was in no sense a man after God's own heart.
Gill treats the verse as the hinge that carries David out of the land.
Ellicott: a wide difference from the former visit — once a lone fugitive feigning madness, now the head of a devoted army useful against Saul; Keil notes the assigned dwellings.
Clarke: 'every man with his household' shows a whole migratory community, like a tribe of wandering Arabs.
Gill: Gath was the royal city, so the anointed of Israel now keeps house in the enemy's capital.
Saul had pursued to the very moment of the flight, then desisted; Ellicott adds Ewald's note on the Gittith psalm-titles learned in this Philistine period.
Gill: Saul kept spies to watch David's motions, and so learned of the flight.

outliers.note: Reads the security through Eastern nomad custom: once a stranger is received into the tent, his claim to protection is established.

schools.summary: Henry: a prudent, modest request; those who would stand fast must not covet to stand high, and nothing is lost by a willingness to retire.
Wesley: a prudent desire to guard his people from Philistine vices and religious envy; the disclaimer 'with thee' is humble courtesy.
Ellicott: the real aim was a base for independent forays, free of Philistine supervision — they had one purpose, David another.

debates.question: What chiefly moved David's request to leave the royal city?

sides.position: devout self-preservation and humility, to guard his people and religion
policy — a private base from which to conduct his own forays

schools.summary: Henry: Israel recovers an ancient right (Ziklag was Judah's, then Simeon's); the righteous God judges righteously and repays humility with an inheritance.
Ziklag, once Simeon's, had fallen to the Philistines and lay uninhabited; the clause 'unto this day' dates the book's composition (Ellicott: after Jeroboam, before the exile).
Gill glosses the site (twelve miles from Gath, per Bunting) and the restoration of the town to Judah.
Wesley: given not merely to inhabit but to possess; thereafter the king of Judah's, David and his heirs' for ever; 'to this day' was added later.

debates.question: What does 'unto this day' show about the book's composition?

sides.position: after Jeroboam's revolt and before the carrying away to Babylon, when 'kings of Judah' named a distinct line
simply long after Samuel, added by a later hand from earlier materials

schools.summary: The exact 'year and four months' (Hebrew yamim = a full term of days) proves the historical character of the narrative — a fabricator does not date to the month.
Gill confirms the reckoning without elaboration.
Henry acquits the war: heaven had doomed these nations and ordained David to rule, so he makes up the obedience Saul withheld toward Amalek — yet not the deceit that follows.
Keil: the unstated occasion is surely these tribes' raids into Israel; Ellicott: 'went up' to the higher plateau against Bedaween scourges reaching to Egypt's frontier.
Wesley: these were the Amalekites who survived Saul's earlier war and had retired into the desert.

debates.question: How is David's raid on these nations to be judged?

sides.position: righteous — the anointed king executing an old divine sentence and completing Saul's unfinished obedience
a ferocious, unauthorized foray whose barbarity the next verses expose

schools.summary: Ellicott: barbarity without excuse; David had no charge for a war of extermination, and this blood may be what unfitted him to build the temple — yet the chronicler's host was even then gathering to him.
Clarke: a complete extirpation, none left to carry tidings to Achish; Wesley: none left only in the part where he came, more Amalekites surviving elsewhere.
Gill records the slaughter and spoil without comment.
Wesley: ambiguous words unbecoming a prince and professor of true religion, aggravated by Achish's trust; a scene David's prayer 'remove from me the way of lying' repents of.
Ellicott: simply a falsehood — David raided Bedaween toward Egypt but named Judahite Jerahmeelites and protected Kenites to deceive his suzerain.
Gill: he imposed on Achish by ambiguous terms, meaning the nations south of Judah, not the south of Judah itself.
Gill softens the scene with Abarbanel — these tribes were enemies of Achish — and reads the point as David's not bringing captives to Gath to contradict his story.

Ellicott, unsparing: atrocious murder in which the wild Arab chief alone appears; coming after Samuel's death, want of trust and craven fear led one sin on to another; the clause 'so did David' is the narrator's remark.

debates.question: How should the killing of all the captives be judged?

sides.position: softened — Abarbanel's view that these were Achish's own enemies; the stress falls on not bringing captives to Gath
atrocious murder to conceal a lie, the fruit of lost faith after Samuel's death

schools.summary: Gill: Achish believed the story, imposed on by ambiguous terms; a man odious to his own nation seems a vassal for ever.
Keil catches the irony of Achish's 'for ever' — a permanence the next chapters overturn as David is delivered to reign.
Clarke: the deception made Achish feel secure in his deadliest enemy; no lie is of the truth, and all falsity is an abomination to the Lord.
